behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is algorithm-quality at a single computation boundary (heuristic output vs optimal baseline) with no OS/network/subprocess/LLM dependency in the behavior text. Rejected Integration because the text does not require cross-module handoff verification; it specifies a direct property of the heuristic itself. Rejected System/Agentic/Workflow because no real external infra, AI model output, or multi-step agent chain is described.
behaviors.test_description: Run deterministic in-process cases (coordinates only) up to 20 stops, compute optimal route length with an exact solver in test code, and assert heuristic_length <= 1.5 * optimal_length.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: The described failure is formula/config application correctness (distance and speed mapping) and can be validated at function/object boundary without real infra. Rejected Integration because behavior text does not explicitly require validating orchestration between independent modules beyond this computation. Rejected System/Agentic/Workflow: no external contract, no AI output dependence, no multi-step agentic flow.
behaviors.test_description: Given fixed consecutive-stop distances and road-type speed config fixtures, assert estimated segment times and aggregate delivery windows equal expected values (including edge cases for mixed road types).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: This is pure validation/arithmetic behavior (compare total estimated time to remaining shift hours and compute excess) at a local boundary. Rejected Integration because behavior text does not require proving cross-module state handoff; it states validator behavior directly. Rejected System/Agentic/Workflow due to no real infra, no model dependence, and no multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Feed validator with total-estimated-time and remaining-shift-hours values around the threshold; assert rejection when exceeded and exact excess value returned.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears at module interaction boundaries: geocoding result handling + stop filtering + optimizer input + skipped-address reporting. Unit-only tests of each part can miss handoff bugs. Rejected System because the behavior text only says "API returns no results" and does not require real external response-shape/contract verification or explicit round-trip external side-effect proof. Rejected Agentic/Workflow: no AI model dependency or multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Use an in-process geocoding stub that returns no result for one address and valid coordinates for others; execute the service end-to-end in process, then assert the failed address is reported as skipped and the optimized route excludes it while remaining stops are routed.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on orchestration across preprocessing (dedupe/merge items) and routing consumption (single visit), so module handoff is the likely false-confidence gap beyond unit tests. Rejected System because no explicit requirement for real external contracts or round-trip external side effects. Rejected Agentic/Workflow because no LLM/ML decision-making or multi-step agentic chain is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service with duplicate-address inputs containing distinct delivery items; verify one consolidated stop is produced, items are combined correctly, and final route contains that address exactly once.
